title: Mythos Gets a Partial Reprieve, Asian Rivals Fill the Vacuum, China Retaliates on Minerals
subhead: The Trump administration allows Anthropic to release Mythos to select US companies and agencies while Japan and China launch competing models, Beijing retaliates against rare-earth firms, and the UK bans under-16s from social media.

sections.heading: Mythos Returns — But Only for Some
sections.body:
The Trump administration has partially reversed its June 12 export control directive, allowing Anthropic to release its Mythos 5 model to select US companies and government agencies, according to CNBC and Bloomberg. The move comes two weeks after the Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to shut down both Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all users, including foreign nationals, citing a narrow jailbreak vulnerability in Fable 5 that could enable cybersecurity misuse. Anthropic said it had received only 'verbal evidence of a potential narrow, non-universal jailbreak' and argued the standard, if applied across the industry, 'would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers.' The partial reprieve keeps the ban in place for all non-US users and for Fable 5 entirely, meaning hundreds of millions of global users still lack access to Anthropic's frontier models.

French President Emmanuel Macron said at the G7 summit in Evian that he expects progress on broadening Mythos access to allied nations, according to Reuters. G7 leaders discussed a 'trusted partners' framework that would grant vetted allies access to cutting-edge US AI models, but the mechanism remains undefined and European officials have privately pushed back on Washington controlling the terms, according to The National and Reuters.
sections.whyItMatters: The partial lift is a tactical retreat, not a reversal. The core precedent — that the US government can unilaterally shutter a frontier model deployed to hundreds of millions — stands. Allied nations are now negotiating access as a privilege, not a market right.

sections.heading: Sakana and 360 Step Into the Mythos Gap
sections.body:
Within days of the US export ban on Mythos and Fable, competitors in Japan and China launched products targeting the vacuum. Tokyo-based Sakana AI released Fugu, a frontier model it says 'stands shoulder-to-shoulder' with Fable 5 and Mythos Preview, optimized for agent orchestration across multiple models via API. Sakana co-founder David Ha described it as a hedge: 'Access to top models can disappear overnight. Collective intelligence is the practical hedge against this concentration of power.' Sakana co-founder Ren Ito, speaking at the G7 in Evian, urged the US to preserve allied access, writing in Project Syndicate that 'AI should not become a technology that is hoarded; it should be one that is developed together.'

Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 unveiled Tulongfeng, a vulnerability-discovery AI it says matches Mythos, alongside Yitianzhen, an automated cyber-defense tool. Founder Zhou Hongyi described vulnerability-finding AI as a 'national strategic asset' and warned of 'one-way transparency' — where some actors get advanced vulnerability detection while others do not, according to Reuters and Quartz. Sakana AI raised $135 million in its Series B at a $2.65 billion valuation last November; Anthropic's run-rate revenue crossed $47 billion in May 2026, per TechCrunch.
sections.whyItMatters: The export control order did not eliminate frontier AI capability — it relocated it. Japan and China each produced competitive alternatives within weeks, and 360 explicitly framed its launch around the strategic vulnerability US policy created.

sections.heading: China Retaliates on Rare Earths as G7 Minerals Plan Stalls
sections.body:
Beijing added 10 US companies — including rare-earth miner MP Materials and rare-earth magnet maker USA Rare Earths — to its export control list on June 22, barring Chinese firms from exporting dual-use items to them. The Ministry of Finance separately blocked government procurement from 46 US companies, including Lockheed Martin, Boeing, General Atomics and General Dynamics subsidiaries. The move came two weeks after the Pentagon blacklisted ~80 Chinese companies, including Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD, as 'military-civil fusion contributors,' according to Al Jazeera.

The retaliation coincides with the G7's struggling effort to build a Western critical minerals trading bloc. Vice President JD Vance's February proposal to use a DARPA-built AI pricing program called OPEN to set floor prices for rare earths, antimony, graphite, and tungsten has met resistance. European allies oppose Washington controlling the pricing mechanism and want market-driven indexes. The EU-funded EIT RawMaterials is working with Metalshub to create alternative pricing indexes outside Chinese government-led pricing. A US proposal for binding bilateral agreements with Japan and the EU covering 5-10 minerals is expected before the end of June, according to CNBC and Reuters.

China's order extends extraterritorially, barring 'foreign institutions and individuals worldwide' from transferring Chinese dual-use goods to the listed companies. Supply chain consultant Cameron Johnson, speaking to Al Jazeera, said the order mirrors US semiconductor controls but may be hard to enforce, as many named firms have already de-risked their Chinese supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: Both sides are now actively weaponizing minerals supply chains. The G7's effort to create an alternative pricing regime is real but divided — Europe wants transparency, not a Pentagon AI setting prices for strategic commodities.

sections.heading: UK Bans Under-16s From Social Media as Global Trend Accelerates
sections.body:
UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a ban on social media use for children under 16, covering Snapchat, TikTok, YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, and X. The ban, expected to take effect in spring 2027, follows Australia's pioneering under-16 social media law from last year. Starmer said enforcement will target platforms, not children, with multimillion-dollar fines for non-compliance — and pledged to go further than Australia's measures. The UK government received 116,000 public responses to its consultation, with over 90% supporting an under-16 ban, per AP News.

YouTube and Meta both warned that a blanket ban could push teens into unregulated, less-safe spaces. A Guardian study found that four in five Australian under-16s are still using social media despite that country's ban. The US Embassy in London publicly opposed the move, warning it could burden American tech companies and conflict with free speech protections. Australia, meanwhile, announced it will double maximum penalties for platforms breaching its ban, per BBC News. France, Spain, Denmark, Thailand, South Korea, Brazil, and Indonesia are all developing or implementing similar restrictions.
sections.whyItMatters: The global under-16 social media ban movement is accelerating but faces an enforcement gap: Australia's experience shows high-level bans without robust age verification push users into less-regulated alternatives, not away from platforms.

sections.heading: US Chip Market in China Is Over, Brookings Concludes
sections.body:
A Brookings Institution analysis published June 17 concluded that 'the US is out of the AI chip market in China', arguing that Chinese authorities now view US chipmakers as unreliable partners and will not allow domestic firms to purchase American chips even when the US approves sales. The analysis noted that the Trump administration approved Nvidia's H200 chip for sale to 10 Chinese companies in May, but not a single H200 had been sold because Beijing blocked the purchases. Trump himself acknowledged that China 'chose not to' buy the chips, saying 'they want to try and develop their own.'

The Brookings analysis warns that three proposed policy expansions — extending chip controls to third countries, excluding Chinese AI models from US and allied markets, and restricting access to Chinese memory chips — would each accomplish 'little except isolating US companies from the global AI and chip marketplace.' Separately, Taiwan's government is considering stricter AI chip export controls to China to align with US restrictions, per UPI and Taipei Times. A Carnegie Endowment analysis has also raised concerns that expanding compute controls to cloud services could push middle-tier nations toward Chinese alternatives, according to Timothy B. Lee's analysis cited by Brookings.
sections.whyItMatters: The decoupling is now mutual and self-reinforcing: the US restricts chip exports, and China refuses to buy even the chips the US allows. The result is a permanently bifurcated semiconductor ecosystem where US firms cede the Chinese market entirely.

sections.heading: Accenture Bets $4.2B on OT Cybersecurity as Infrastructure Threats Rise
sections.body:
Accenture announced it is acquiring a majority stake in Dragos and all of runZero and NetRise in a combined $4.175 billion deal targeting operational technology (OT) cybersecurity — the systems controlling power grids, water treatment, manufacturing, and other critical infrastructure. The acquisitions will be folded into Accenture's cybersecurity practice and signal the consulting giant's view that AI-driven cyber threats and geopolitical risk are creating durable demand for OT-specific defenses, per Accenture's announcement and CyberScoop.

RUSI reported that 75% of cyber attacks on UK critical infrastructure are now attributed to hostile states, per NCSC CEO testimony. Meanwhile, the White House's state infrastructure cybersecurity initiative has stalled, according to Cybersecurity Dive, and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is pushing new regulations for critical infrastructure incident reporting and AI-era risk management. A global Europol operation disrupted the SocGholish, Amadey, and StealC malware networks last week, showing coordinated enforcement capacity but also the scale of the criminal infrastructure ecosystem.
sections.whyItMatters: The private sector is filling the gap left by stalled government cybersecurity mandates. Accenture's $4.2B bet reflects a conviction that OT security is becoming a structural need, not a discretionary spend.

sections.heading: Türkiye: AI Regulation and the Export Control Ripple Effect
sections.body: While Turkey was not directly targeted by this week's AI or minerals export controls, the ripple effects are tangible. As a NATO member with significant defense procurement from US firms now on China's export control list, Turkey faces potential supply chain disruption on dual-use components. Turkey's own AI sector remains small compared to the US, China, and Japan, but the global trend toward AI sovereignty — countries requiring local models to reduce dependence on US providers — mirrors debates in Ankara about strategic autonomy in digital infrastructure. The UK social media ban debate also resonates in Turkey, where platform regulation has been a recurring political issue and where the government has previously imposed temporary social media throttling during crises. Singapore's 38.4% export surge in May, driven by AI-related chip demand, illustrates how global semiconductor supply chains continue to flow through intermediaries — a model Turkey could seek to replicate as it expands its own electronics manufacturing base.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is not yet a major AI actor, but the weaponization of AI access and chip supply chains creates both risk and opportunity for a country that straddles NATO and Middle Eastern trade routes.

bottomLine: The week ending June 28 crystallized a shift: US AI export controls did not eliminate frontier capability but redistributed it, with Japan and China each fielding Mythos-competitive models within days. China matched US escalation on minerals, targeting rare-earth firms with extraterritorial export controls. The G7's proposed alternative minerals pricing regime is real but divided between a US-backed Pentagon AI model and European demands for market-driven indexes. Meanwhile, the UK joined the global under-16 social media ban movement, Accenture placed a $4.2B bet that OT cybersecurity is now structural infrastructure, and Brookings formally called the end of the US chip market in China — noting Beijing now refuses to buy even the chips Washington approves for sale.
sources: TechCrunch, CNBC, Bloomberg, CNA, AP News, Al Jazeera, BBC News, Nikkei Asia, Brookings Institution, CyberScoop, SecurityWeek, RUSI, Cybersecurity Dive, The National, Reuters, Quartz, Guardian, Fortune, Forbes, NPR, Europol, Accenture
